questions.text: Rate the extent to which the Constitution is accepted as providing rules that everyone in government should follow.
questions.info_link:
As an indication, if challenges are frequent and Constitutional amendments are common and easy to carry out, then rate low; if they are uncommon even if they are relatively easy to accomplish, rate high.
In a minimal sense of the term, a "constitution" consists of a set of rules or norms creating, structuring and defining the limits of government power or authority. Constitutions specify the limits on the three basic forms of government power: legislative power (making new laws), executive power (implementing laws) and judicial power (adjudicating disputes under laws). These rules not only create legislative, executive and judicial powers, but also impose limits on them. They are expressed in the form of individual or group rights against government, rights to free expression, association, equality and due process of law. They can also characterize the scope of authority (e.g., in a federal system, provincial or state governments may have authority over health care and education while the federal government's jurisdiction extends to national defense and transportation); the mechanisms used in exercising the relevant power (e.g., procedural requirements governing the form and manner of legislation); and civil rights (e.g., in a Charter or Bill of Rights).
See http://plato.stanford.edu/entries/constitutionalism/ for a philosophical discussion on constitutionalism.
This question is essentially asking you to rate how well-established these rules have become in your country. In many mature democracies, the constitution is regarded as the primary source of legitimization, primarily because it has stood the test of time by instituting limits on government power while providing rights to citizens. Can the same be said of the constitution in your country? Or, is it a document that is either widely seen as irrelevant or has gone through major revisions relatively recently and has therefore not acquired peopleĺs confidence?
A rich directory of internet sources relating to constitutional law can be found at http://www.lib.uchicago.edu/~llou/conlaw.html.

comments.comment: Here's the rationale: Evidence supporting moderate constitutional adherence: The Brazilian Supreme Federal Court (STF) has demonstrated robust enforcement of constitutional principles in several recent high-profile cases. In 2024, the Court unanimously ruled that the Armed Forces cannot serve as a "moderating power" between branches of government, firmly upholding the constitutional separation of powers principle. In September 2025, the Senate rejected the controversial "Shielding Constitutional Amendment" after nationwide mobilization and judicial scrutiny, showing that constitutional guardrails can withstand attempts at self-dealing by legislators. The Court has also intervened decisively in platform regulation and anti-corruption matters, using constitutional authority to fill regulatory gaps. Evidence of significant compliance gaps: Brazil's performance on international governance indices reveals substantial rule of law deficits. The 2024 World Justice Project Rule of Law Index ranks Brazil 80th out of 142 countries (score 0.50, below the global average of 0.55). More troubling, Brazil ranks 113th in criminal justice with a dismal judicial impartiality score of 0.10 - tied with Venezuela. Transparency International's 2024 Corruption Perceptions Index gave Brazil a score of 34/100, its worst result since 2012, ranking 107th globally. The legislative branch ranks as the second most corrupt globally after Haiti. The attempted rush of the "Shielding Constitutional Amendment" through Congress with procedural irregularities - approved by 314 deputies in just two days without public participation - illustrates how constitutional rules can be systematically evaded, even if ultimately blocked. This pattern suggests that while the judiciary enforces constitutional boundaries, significant portions of government actors test those boundaries aggressively. A score of 4 reflects a system where constitutional rules provide meaningful constraints enforced by an active judiciary, but where substantial portions of government actors regularly attempt to circumvent or ignore those rules, particularly in areas with weak oversight.

The 1945 Constitution, after the post-Suharto Reformasi period, has come to be widely followed. Bodies such as the 'Constitutional Court' aid this. However, political pressure and resistance from popular figures implies not everyone follows it strictly.

Bulgaria has a well defined legal status to constitution adopted in 1991, and has a parliamentary democracy which outlines the basic rights and freedoms of citizens. Yet, there have been instances where political actors have been accused of circumventing constitutional limits for political gain. It is also one of the lowest performing EU Countries as per Transparency International's Corruption Perceptions Index due to the problems with rule of law and oligarchy influences.

In Sweden, the Constitution is highly respected and serves as the cornerstone for governance. Government bodies and officials strictly adhere to its rules, and deviations are rare, as the rule of law is deeply embedded in Swedish political culture.

questions.text: Rate the extent to which the Constitution is ammended arbitrarily by the government of the day or by agreement between major political groups.
questions.info_link:
If the government in power has the ability to easily change the Constitution without widespread agreement from many political groups, rate low. If the Constitution is only changed after a consensus has been built across all major political groups, rate high.
Since a strong Constitution is both relatively immutable and protective of minority rights, the government in power should not be able to amend it without the support of a wide range of political groups. Checks against such practices are written into many national Constitutions, and range from supermajority requirements of three-fifths to three-fourths of the vote in order to adopt an amendment, or layered procedures requiring successive national and/or regional confirmations, in some cases followed by citizen's referendums.
You may be able to find information on the extent to which your countryĺs Constitution is protected from arbitrary amendment procedures reviewing its contents at http://confinder.richmond.edu/.

comments.comment: Post Reformasi, political consensus has become paramount for passing any amendments. The series of amendments made from 1999 to 2002, for example, depict this. Consistent pressure from the political elite does pose a challenge, but the amendments are by no means 'arbitrary'

Bulgaria's constitution has a well laid out system for carrying out amendments. For example, high threshold of majority for passing a motion, less frequency of amendment since 1991, a requirement of multi-party system necessitating a broad agreement, and judicial oversight reviewing the constitutionality of laws. Yet, there have been some minor instances of political processes leading to compromises.

Constitutional amendments in Sweden are typically subject to broad political agreement, often requiring multiple parliamentary sessions and consensus. While political consensus is usually reached, there are concerns about potential majoritarian changes in specific cases.
There is a two times hearing process, to amend the constitution, and sometimes requires a public referendum. For the hearing it requires at least 75% of the votes to pass it.

questions.text: Rate the extent to which the Constitution protects individual human rights.
questions.info_link:
If individual and human rights are not seen as being of primary importance, rate low; otherwise rate higher, based on the extent to which rights acquire prominence and are well protected.
One of the most important substantial features of a countryĺs constitution have to do with the rights it confers on individuals. By giving individuals certain fundamental rights, such as the right to form associations, the right to free speech, and the right to equal protection, the constitution essentially protects them from arbitrary actions by government power.
You may be able to find information on the extent to which your countryĺs constitution protects individual rights by browsing through the ôComparative Constitutional Rights Chartö at http://www.hrcr.org/chart/. See also for other possible sources of information on your countryĺs constitution, whose text you may find at http://confinder.richmond.edu/.

comments.comment: After the fall of Suharto, the Second Amendment (2000) introduced a 'Comprehensive Bill of Rights' which includes the right to life, freedom of expression, equality before law, etc. The controversial 'ITE' law, however, prevents citizens from 'insulting' state officials, running against the right to freedom of expression. There exists a gap between Constitutional provisions and on-ground implementation of these rights too.
The Bulgarian Constitution provides a strong foundation for human rights protection on paper. However, there are gaps between constitutional provisions and their practical implementation.

Swedenĺs Constitution provides robust protection of individual human rights. The countryĺs legal framework is aligned with international human rights norms, and its implementation is generally effective, with strong judicial oversight ensuring these rights are upheld.
A solid legal framework exists, though there are shortcomings in its implementation

questions.text: Rate the extent to which the Constitution is especially protective of rights to minority populations.
questions.info_link:
Rate high if the rights of minority populations are specifically protected in the Constitution. Rate low if the issue of minority rights is absent from the Constitution, or if majority populations are explicitly granted special status by the Constitution.
Along with protecting individuals, a constitution is also typically charged with protecting minorities from the "tyranny of the majority," in John Stuart Millĺs phrase (http://www.bartleby.com/130/). As a principle, the fundamental rights of individuals in the minority are protected. This could also include groups that may not necessarily constitute the numerical minority, but who have been historically persecuted, or for other reasons have relatively low social and economic standing and, correspondingly, have little or no political representation. You can find more on minority rights at http://www.minorityrights.org.
In older constitutions, like those of the US, minority rights are not explicitly laid out, but have been developed through civil rights legislation. In other countries, such as South Africa and India (and through revision in Canada), these rights have become embedded in the constitution (see http://www.hrcr.org/chart/group/minorities.html). You should use your judgment to decide how to provide a rating to answer this question, even if the constitution is not explicit on the issue of minority rights. Thus, if your country does not have written constitutional provisions to protect minorities, but nevertheless has a well-defined body of case law that establishes rights to racial and ethnic minorities, women, and other historically under-privileged groups, give a high score; otherwise a low one.

comments.comment: There exist Constitutional provisions for protection of religious and ethnic minorities, women, etc. However, implementation is a problem and there exist some parts of the country where constitutional protections have not been fully realised. eg: Though Indonesia recognizes LGBTQ members as equal, some provinces are governed under Sharia law, and same-sex relationships are criminalized.

While there are no explicit provision for minority groups in the constitution, there are certain case laws such as Protection Against Discrimination Act (2004), National Roma Integration Strategy, etc.

The Swedish Constitution includes protections for minority rights, especially in the context of linguistic and cultural minorities. While these protections are fairly strong, some critics argue that there is still room for improvement, particularly regarding the Sami population.

Steps are being taken towards protecting ethnic minorities. However, the groups are discriminated against and harassed. They face employment issues. Also, there is no rule for the identification and protection of the LGBTQ+ community.

questions.text: Does the Constitution provide adequate procedures for assuring the accountability of all the institutions of government?
questions.info_link:
If the Constitution specifically enumerates how and to whom different institutions (i.e. courts, legislature, military) will be held accountable for their actions, rate high. If the Constitution is silent on this issue, rate low.
Governmental accountability is a critical element of any country operating under the rule of law. A robust Constitution will both divide power among several institutions and describe the process by which each of these institutions, as well as their members, are held accountable for their actions. Note that the division of powers should be protected to some extent throughout any accountability process. For instance, in many countries, only the Legislature itself may investigate and prosecute its own members for criminal or ethical violations, which protects this institution from domination by the Executive or the Courts. This situation provides a stark division of powers, but may not provide excellent accountability, since a legislator of a majority party may receive favorable treatment in an investigation. On the other hand, a Constitution may lay out the procedure by which the Executive leader may be impeached, but accountability may not be assured if this involves multiple steps through courts and legislative bodies that are allied with or beholden to the Executive.
You may be able to find more about your selected country's Constitution at the International Constitutional Law Center's website (http://www.oefre.unibe.ch/law/icl/.

comments.comment: The post-Reformasi period has seen major democratic reforms that push for a chain accountability within arms of the government such as the executive, judiciary, legislature, etc. These checks are based on ideas of decentralization, anti-corruption, judicial review, etc.

The Bulgarian Constitution does provide a basis for equal rights and non-discrimination. However, the rating is not higher because it doesn't explicitly recognize or provide special protections for minorities.

The Constitution provides clear accountability mechanisms, particularly through the principles of transparency and public access to government information. These are strongly upheld, although there are occasional criticisms regarding the effectiveness of oversight bodies.

questions.text: To what extent does the Constitution provide adequate checks and balances through the division of powers?
questions.info_link:
If the checks and balances are prevalent and provide sound division of powers, rate high; if it checks and balances are ambiguous or absent, rate low.
The most basic reason that a Constitution would provide for a separation of powers among several independent but overlapping branches of government is to assure that no one branch of government can gain control over the others. In this context, such a separation will help to ensure the independence of the several spheres of government, insulating 'just' spheres from 'corrupt' ones, while making accountability of those 'corrupt' spheres possible. When powers are divided, a sphere of government that is wrought with corruption has a lower likelihood of infecting other spheres with similar problems, since they may have differing leaderships, moral culture, access to resources, and the like. Also, a corrupt Executive will be unable to use the Courts or Legislature for illicit gains if Executive power is checked and thereby forbidden from use within those spheres.

comments.comment: The legislative, executive and judiciary are designed to keep each other under check. eg: The legislature keeps the executive under check through the 'Right to Question (Art. 20A). The 'Constitutional Courts' of Indonesia, a post-Reformasi phenomenon, check the constitutional validity of laws passed by the executive and the legislature. The Constitutional Court also plays a key role in Presidential impeachment.
Key Constitutional Provisions for Checks and Balances are available through separation of powers, legislative, Executive Branch, and Judicial Branch, Parliamentary powers and local self government.
The constitution provides key mechanisms such as separations of powers, regulatory oversight, judicial review, ombudsman, local self-government, and the emphasis on audit institutions.

Swedenĺs division of powers provides for strong checks and balances, with an independent judiciary, legislative controls, and executive oversight. The system functions effectively with minimal interference across branches, ensuring governance remains balanced.

questions.text: How effective are the procedures for enforcing the rules of the Constitution?
questions.info_link:
Rate high, for instance, if violation of human rights as protected by the Constitution leads to appropriate reprisal; rate low if it does not. Note that this is generally a measure of the extent to which the Constitution expresses its most significant rules consistently and with sufficient weight.
To ensure the protection of rights, the division of powers, the accountability of government and continued consensual development of the Constitution, there must be effective procedures of enforcement. While a Constitution may provide for all the necessary elements of a just and accountable system of government, the rule of these laws may not stand if there are no written procedures for their enforcement. Note that powerful individuals and interest groups may try to circumvent the enforcement of certain rules, and the extent to which citizens either ignore or oppose this tendency. A robust constitution should foster a "Constitutional culture", as noted by Stefan Voigt, where the people recognize the Constitution not as a "book of hopes" but as a legal document that should be universally applied and respected.

comments.comment: There do exist structural problems in the judiciary and law-enforcement agencies of Indonesia. Fueled by corruption and political interference, this can cause ineffective adherence to Constitutional provisions. eg: The Supreme Court is known to favor elites by way of inconsistent decision-making. Systemic corruption within the police force has been hard to eradicate. Human rights violations are common among various law-enforcement bodies of Indonesia.
According to reports from World Justice Report, Bulgaria has an average performance when it comes to enforcing civil justice and criminal justice, and maintenance of order and security.

Enforcement of constitutional rules in Sweden is generally effective, particularly through an independent judiciary and constitutional court. However, certain bureaucratic delays or inefficiencies in enforcement mechanisms can occur, though they are not common.

questions.text: Has the Constitution endured for several years and remained relatively stable?
questions.info_link:
If so, rate high; if unstable, rate low. If you are unsure about this question because the Constitution is recent as a result of an important political transition, leave as NA.
A Constitution which changes regularly may be particularly vulnerable to ill-advised ammendments which are more reflective of the politics of the day than of the spirit of the country and of the rule of law. By its nature, a Constitution differs from other forms of rules in that it is intended to evolve in a manner reflective of the values of the society to which it applies over an extended period of time.
Constitutions which are frequently changed or repealed may also be reflective of what Stefan Voigt called a lack of a "Constitutional culture", wherein all members of the society see the Constitution as a legal document which should be enforced. (See Stefan Voigt's paper on Constitutions at http://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=132288.) A Constitution which is frequently changed might be seen as a model or ideal conceptualization of the society, not as a legal document which describes the resoned expectations of the populace.

comments.comment: Post-Suharto and post-Reformasi, the Constitution has been relatively stable. This is primarily due to effective distribution of powers, and lack of room for arbitrary amendment.

Yes, to a great extent the constitution has been stable since 1991.It has remained the fundamental law of the land despite political changes. There have been amendments, but the basic structure has remained intact.

Swedenĺs Constitution has been stable for decades, with its basic structure enduring since the mid-20th century. While amended to meet contemporary needs, the Constitution remains a strong and stable legal framework for governance.

The Constitution, established in 1995, is relatively new. While it underwent numerous amendments during periods of political turmoil, its core principles have remained intact. Since 2017, it has achieved greater stability.

questions.text: Rate the legitimacy of the process through which legislators are chosen.
questions.info_link:
If the process is through elections, rate their fairness. If some other consensual approach is used, rate its overall public acceptability.
The legislature is one of the lynchpins of governance for any country. The members of the legislature are expected to represent diverse interests within the country and express these points of view while making up laws for the country. Legislatures are typically formed via direct elections, but there may be other mechanisms as well: nominations from local councils; deliberative forums drawn by lot; and so on. What is important is that the rules for forming the legislature are impartial and credible, in that they are consistently applied and are monitored by independent regulatory agencies.
Thus, in an electoral system, simply permitting opposition groups access to the ballot is not enough. They should also be provided access to radio, newspaper and television coverage, perhaps by public funding free air time to election advertisements from all parties. In order for democratic elections to be fair and competitive, opposition parties and candidates must enjoy the rights to freedom of speech, assembly, and movement as necessary to voice their criticisms of the government openly and to bring alternative policies and candidates to the voters. Similarly, opposition party politicians should not be harassed or have their events disrupted.
Consider all these elements to answer this question. You may also want to refer to a variety of resources to get specific information on past elections in your country. See, for instance, http://www.electionguide.org or http://www.electionworld.org. The Lijphart Elections Archive (http://dodgson.ucsd.edu/lij/) has more detailed information on election results and enabling legislation for several countries. The Electoral Reform Society has a good deal of information on electoral systems (http://www.electoral-reform.org.uk/votingsystems/systems.htm).

comments.comment: Elections to the legislative bodies of DRC are fairer than the presidential elections, yet there has been recorded instances of vote buying, creating a ruckus in polling booths and so on. Eastern provinces are so much affected by armed conflicts that elections couldn't be held. But also the CENI has debarred some candidates from contesting the elections due to corruption charges(most were oppositions).
Post Reformasi, Indonesia has held numerous elections, which have been regarded as 'democratic, free and fair'. Elections are overseen by independent bodies.

The basic structure of the electoral system is sound and allows for fair representation. Regular elections are held and monitored by international observers. There is significant voter participation. However, there have been persistent concerns about vote-buying, some political instability, and issues with media independence.

Sweden's election process is widely regarded as fair and transparent, with proportional representation ensuring a broad range of political voices are heard. Elections are conducted freely, and international observers often commend the integrity of the electoral process.

The condition and effectiveness of legislatures in Egypt: Egypt has a bicameral legislature consisting of two chambers: 1. The House of Representatives (lower house) 2. The Senate (upper house, re-established in 2020) Some key points about the Egyptian legislature's condition and effectiveness: 1. Limited independence: The legislature is often seen as having limited independence from the executive branch, particularly the presidency. The current president, Abdel Fattah el-Sisi, wields significant power. 2. Dominated by pro-government forces: The majority of seats in both chambers are held by parties and individuals supportive of the government, limiting robust debate and opposition voices. 3. Constitutional changes: In 2019, constitutional amendments were passed extending presidential term limits and increasing presidential control over the judiciary, which some observers view as further weakening legislative oversight. 4. Rubber-stamp perception: Critics often characterize the legislature as a "rubber stamp" for executive decisions rather than an effective check on presidential power. 5. Limited legislative initiative: Most bills originate from the executive branch rather than being initiated by legislators themselves. 6. Restricted civil liberties: Human rights organizations have raised concerns about restrictions on freedom of speech and assembly, which can impact the ability of opposition voices to participate fully in the legislative process. 7. Some attempts at reform: There have been efforts to increase women's representation in the legislature, with quotas established for female lawmakers. 8. Economic focus: The legislature has been involved in passing economic reforms, often in line with IMF recommendations, though the extent of genuine debate on these issues is questioned by some analysts. Despite these challenges, the Egyptian legislature does play a role in the country's governance, even if its effectiveness as an independent branch of government is limited compared to legislatures in more robust democracies.

questions.text: How representative are members of the legislature of the variety of interests in the society?
questions.info_link: If members of the legislature represent a broad spectrum of social and economic interests, rate high. If the interests of large numbers of citizens are not represented in the legislature, rate low.

comments.comment: Despite being one of the most ethnically diverse countries, DRC's legislative members are dominated by certain powerful groups(from Kinhasa, Katanga, Kivu). People from the lower social and economic classes also do not find proper representation. Women's position is in a clear sorry state with only 12% seats in the assembly.

Indonesia is a diverse archipelago of over 270 million people. Though ensuring that all voices are heard at the national legislature is difficult, such groups are represented at the local self-government level. National interests of certain sections of society, however, remain uncatered to. The rich and elite exert undue political influence at times.

The passing of anti LGBTQ+ bill and underrepresentation of women in the parliament. There have also been instances of oligarchy that have influenced voting in smaller municipalities and within marginalized groups.

Swedenĺs legislature is relatively representative of societal interests, including gender and minority rights. However, concerns remain about the underrepresentation of certain marginalized groups, such as immigrants or indigenous populations like the Sami.

questions.text: Rate the extent to which the legislature can be arbitrarily dissolved by executive fiat.
questions.info_link:
Other than elections, there may be rules that govern changes in the legislative composition through courts or executive fiat. If such rules are poorly designed or poorly and frequently applied, give a low rating. Alternatively, if they are well-designed and rarely applied, give a high rating.
In some countries, the Executive retains the power to dissolve the Legislature. In cases where this power is frequently utilized, the Legislature may become ineffective, since its actions can be pre-emptively nullified by the Executive. In order to draft and impement legislation that most accurately reflects the people's will, these two branches of government should be oblidged to cooperate and able to oversee, though not control, each other. The power to dissolve the Legislature is reserved for the Executive in many parliamentary systems; however, it is often mitigated to some extent by the Legislatureĺs ability to remove the Executive through a vote of no confidence. For more information Executive-Legislative relations, see http://www.undp.org/governance/parldev/docs/govern.htm.

comments.comment: DRC's legislature has been dissolved before during Mobutu's era. But post reforms (2006 constitution), the executive's power to dissolve the legislature is restrictive, yet it has been used as a bargaining tool time and again. Also, the president has replaced the legislative members of eastern provinces during armed conflicts with military representatives.
Post-Reformasi, arbitrary dissolution of the legislature by the executive is impossible.

According to the constitutional provisions, there is very little room for arbitrary dissolution of the legislature by executive fiat in Bulgaria. The constitutional and political system has been designed to prevent such arbitrary actions, aligning with democratic principles of separation of powers and checks and balances.

Swedenĺs legislature can only be dissolved under very specific constitutional rules, such as a vote of no confidence. Arbitrary dissolution by the executive is almost unheard of, reflecting the strong democratic traditions.

questions.text: Rate the extent to which legislators are required to abide by ethical standards.
questions.info_link: If there are strict ethical standards that the legislators regularly abide by, rate high. If no standards exist, or if standards are ignored and there is for other legislators to hold insubordinate members accountable to those standards, rate low.

comments.comment: In DRC, it is formally required to abide by ethical standards but the enforcement mechanism is weak.

Constitutional enforcement of ethical standards for legislators exists, but its practical enforcement remains ambivalent. Enforcement agencies often succumb to political pressure, and political allegiances of legislators often help them get away (or get punished more severely).
On paper, Bulgaria has a strict ethical standards with regards to corruption practices as part of requirements by the EU Council.

Swedish legislators are required to abide by ethical standards, and these are generally seen as fair and well-enforced. However, occasional lapses have led to public debates on strengthening ethical oversight in political processes.

questions.text: Rate the extent to which the legislative decision-making process transparent to the public.
questions.info_link: If debates over important issues are generally open to scrutiny, with written records and access, rate high. If the decision-making process is closed to the public, rate low.

comments.comment: Although it's mandated formally in DRC's constitution for transparency, most important decisions are held behind closed doors. There is also a lack of digital infrastructure to access legal documents. But assembly meetings are held in public though.

The digital outreach of parliamentary proceedings in Indonesia is high. Some sittings of the House are live-streamed, and digital archives of Parliamentary proceedings are available for public use. The general public can access the DPR (legislature's) website and propose topics for legislation. Various citizen feedback forums also exist.

The legislative decision-making process is highly transparent in Sweden, with parliamentary debates, committee meetings, and documents generally accessible to the public. This openness is a core feature of Swedenĺs governance model.

questions.text: How transparent are campaign fund-raising activities and how effective are the rules that govern them?
questions.info_link: Rate high if activities are transparent and the rules are effective; rate low if they are secretive/ineffective.

comments.comment: In DRC, legal frameworks do mandate public disclosure regarding campaign fund-risings, yet again the enforcement part is weak. But if the concerned parties do disclose publicly, it'll be more likely due to international pressure (IMF, World bank group, et al).

Law No.7 of the 2017 amendment is the primary legal framework for campaign financing in Indonesia. Public disclosure of sources of campaign financing is mandated. Campaign financing is supervised by the independent Election Commission (KPU), with the anti-corruption body (KPK) also playing a key role. However, there is a need for greater public awareness and education on campaign financing norms, so as to increase public scrutiny.

There are significant provisions for transparency, including open plenary sessions and publication of laws and voting records. The existence of online platforms for accessing legislative information is positive. Public consultations, while not always extensive, do exist as a mechanism for public input. However, there are limitations in the openness of some committee meetings, concerns about media independence affecting public information, and potential improvements needed in lobbying transparency and the user-friendliness of online information platforms.

While Sweden has rules governing campaign finance, there have been debates about increasing transparency in certain areas, particularly regarding donations to political parties. The system is moderately transparent, but more stringent rules could improve it.

questions.text: Does the legislature have the power to prevent the government from ruling by executive decree or executive orders?
questions.info_link: If the legislature has no checks on the decree of the Executive, rate low. Rate high if the legislature has the power and to do so.

comments.comment: Formally, DRC's legislature can place checks on the executive decrees/orders, but being a semi-presidential monarchy, the president exercises significant amount of powers to overshadow the legislature. Also, the political state in which DRC exists demands ruling by decrees, like the constant threats to the security of the nation by rebel groups regarding ethnicity, resource management,etc. (declaring of the "state of seige").
Yes, post-Reformassi Indonesia has seen robust measures in this regard. The checks the legislature has over the executive ensure this does not occur.

The Swedish legislature has robust mechanisms to prevent the government from ruling by executive decree. The Riksdag (parliament) plays a critical role in reviewing executive actions, ensuring that checks on executive power remain strong.

questions.text: How transparent are the budgetmaking decisions of the legislature?
questions.info_link: Rate low if this process is secretive or impossible to disipher; rate high if this process is transparent.

comments.comment: The Public Finance Law of DRC mandates transparency in budget allocations. Also, international partners pressure DRC's govt. for public disclosure to ensure their financial aids are not disappearing into thin air. Yet, weak oversight, executive dominance and complexity of the nature of the budget still pose a threat to it's transparency.

Budget-making sessions of Indonesian parliament are highly transparent, with public access to every document and discussion taking place in the legislature during this time. Legislative scrutiny and public feedback avenues also exist.

Budget-making decisions in Sweden are transparent, with detailed documentation and processes available for public scrutiny. This level of transparency ensures accountability and minimizes the likelihood of corruption or mismanagement.

questions.text: Rate the role of the Legislature in declaring/ratifying Executive decisions relating to emergency powers and war.
questions.info_link: Rate low if the Legislature plays little or no role and high if its involvement is necessary.

comments.comment: Legislature's approval is necessary in ratifying executive decisions in DRC, yet it has scrutinised for lack of thorough debate before ratifying. The legislature usually supports the executive's decision in this regard due to the state of insecurity prevalent in the eastern provinces (State of Siege, Military operations against insurgency).

Though the President can call a state emergency almost autonomously, budgetary approvals (especially for long-term implications of the emergency) must come through the legislative council. Post-emergency, the legislature also has the power to review all actions of the executive during the emergency. However, since the political alignment of the legislature is with the executive, it can lead to insufficient oversight. The paucity of time can also be a cause of lack of legislative oversight.

The National Assembly of Bulgaria plays a central role in the declaration of war and the ratification of executive decisions related to emergency powers. This legislative oversight ensures that actions of significant national consequence, like war or emergency declarations, are subject to democratic scrutiny and are not solely in the hands of the executive branch.

The Swedish legislature is significantly involved in decisions related to emergency powers and war. However, there is a strong tradition of executive discretion in urgent matters, though the legislature must still ratify major decisions.

questions.text: Rate the extent to which there is criminal influence over the Legislature.
questions.info_link: A low rating denotes pervasive criminal influence over legislators, while a high rating denotes little to none.

comments.comment: There are lot of reports indicating criminal influence in DRC's legislature. Instances of legislative members receiving bribes or getting a share of profit by providing illegal access to mineral resources have been reported. Some legislations have also been put on hold that may hinder the access to resources. Political figures (ex.- Henri Mova Sakanyi) have been reported to have ties with criminal networks and armed groups.

Corruption remains a significant issue in Indonesian politics. Steering legislative decisions through bribes is not uncommon. Though episodes of bribery exist, they seem to be sporadic instances rather than reflective of a nexus of organized crime - which is a good sign. Indonesia is continuously strengthening reforms on all fronts to combat criminal influence over the legislature.

Criminal influence over the Bulgarian legislature is a concern due to corruption, oligarchic influence, and the historical weakness of anti-corruption institutions. Although there have been efforts to address these issues, they have not fully eradicated the problem. The extent of criminal influence is still moderate to high, but recent public and international pressure could drive reforms to reduce this over time. The situation remains fluid, and further developments in Bulgaria's political and legal landscape will determine the extent to which this influence is curtailed.

Criminal influence over the Swedish legislature is almost nonexistent. Strong laws, transparency, and institutional safeguards ensure that the political process remains free from corruption or criminal manipulation.

questions.text: How strong is the influence on the legislature of special interests?
questions.info_link: Rate low if special interests are strong to the point of dictating the policy decisions of the legislature; rate high if special interests are weak.

comments.comment: Special interest groups like Mining and extraction sector has a significant influence over DRC's legislature. They are so influential, due to their lobbying and campaign fund-rising activities, that the legislature had to roll back on it's "Mining Code" provisions to reflect more of their interests, which was initially intended as a reform.

Special interest groups do have some influence on the legislature, especially through lobbying efforts. However, Sweden has rules in place to limit undue influence, and the power of these interests is generally seen as moderate compared to other countries.

questions.text: Do changes in the leadership of the Executive occur according to established rules?
questions.info_link:
If, for instance, the process through which the Executive leader is changed occurs according to direct elections, rate their overall fairness. If some other consentual approach is used, rate its overall public acceptability.
In a democracy, the Executive leader is usually the President or Prime Minister. The functions of the specific offices may vary, but in most cases, the Executive branch of the government is responsible for the day-to-day operation of governmental departments and services, the execution and enforcement of laws, the conduct of foreign policy, command of the armed forces and in some cases the issuing of executive orders or decrees. The leader of the Executive branch, while ideally supported by many capable public officials and advisors, ultimately commands more authority than any other single public servant in a country. For this reason, it is crucial that the office of the Executive changes hands in according to regular, predictable and consistent rules. Direct elections of the Executive leader should therefore be free and fair. Terms should not extend beyond several years before some consentual confirmation or rejection of the office holder occurs. In the event that the citizens clearly desire a change in Executive leadership, there should be a process by which this can happen in a stable and transparent manner.
The CIA World Factbook contains a comprehensive list of the heads of state and cabinets of every country, which can be found here: http://www.cia.gov/cia/publications/chiefs/index.html

comments.comment: Indonesia's 2014 Civil Service Law made the appointment of civil servants more merit-based, with promotions based on competency. However, political challenges remain and extent of implementation varies from region to region.

Leadership changes do not always occur according to established rules. There are legal frameworks in place, but not strictly adhered to. Political manoeuvring, patronage, and corruption are prevalent factors that influence the succession process.

The President is elected for a four-year term and can serve a maximum of two consecutive terms. If a president resigns, is impeached, or dies, the Vice President assumes office. New elections are then held if the vacancy occurs in the last two years of the presidential term. The process is governed by specific legal and procedural guidelines to ensure stability and continuity in governance.

Changes in Sweden's executive leadership follow established constitutional rules. Leadership transitions occur according to democratic procedures, such as general elections or parliamentary votes, ensuring stability and rule of law.

questions.text: To what extent are the leaders of the executive sphere chosen as the result of free and fair elections?
questions.info_link:
If free and fair elections are held, rate high; if leaders are chosen by some other non-consentual process, or if there are no elections at all, rate low.
Note that in some cases, the Executive leader may not be chosen directly by the citizenry. In the US, for example, the Executive is chosen by the Electoral College on behalf of the constituent states, while in most parliamentary systems, the Executive is the head of the majority legislative party.

comments.comment: Since the fall of the Suharto regime, the country has improved in democratisation; its elections are now widely seen as competitive and transparent. However, there have been criticisms regarding voter fraud, campaign finance irregularities, and the influence of money politics.

The electoral process is overseen by the Superior Electoral Court (TSE), which ensures compliance with democratic standards. Elections are conducted via a secret ballot, and various political parties participate. However, challenges such as campaign financing, political corruption, and occasional allegations of voter manipulation can impact perceptions of fairness. Overall, while the electoral framework is designed to be democratic, issues can arise that complicate the integrity of the process.

Executive leaders are chosen through free and fair elections in Sweden. The Prime Minister is selected by the Riksdag (parliament) after general elections, which are internationally recognized for their integrity and fairness.

Elections seem to be free and fair as each election witnesses differences in votes acquired with no party winning the majority. However, acc. to Gallup international survey only 21% of Bulgarians believe in free&fair election

questions.text: Is the head of government's term of office for life or are their constitutional limits or the need for periodic endorsement through elections?
questions.info_link: Rate high if periodic endorsement occurs through end-of-term elections, popular referendum, recall, votes of confidence or some other method. Rate low if the leader of the executive sphere serves a life-long term without review.

comments.comment: Parliamentary elections in Georgia are held every four years, with the next election scheduled for October 26, 2024. The Prime Minister, as the head of the executive branch, can remain in office indefinitely, provided their party continues to win elections and the Parliament approves their nomination for the position.

The head of government in Indonesia, the President, is not elected for life. Their term of office is limited to two consecutive five-year terms. This means that the President must be elected every five years and can only serve a maximum of two terms.

The head of government in Brazil, the President, does not serve for life. The presidential term is set at four years, and a president can serve a maximum of two consecutive terms. After two terms, they must step down and cannot run for re-election immediately.

Sweden's Prime Minister serves a term that requires periodic endorsement through elections. There are no lifetime terms, and leaders must maintain parliamentary confidence, reflecting strong democratic norms.

questions.text: Are there clear procedures for dismissing the executive for major violations of the law?
questions.info_link: If impeachment or another similar removal process is nearly impossible; if rules are poorly designed or used inappropriately, rate low. Rate high if impeachment rules are well designed and carefully exercised.

comments.comment: The process involves impeachment, which can be initiated by a two-thirds majority vote in the Chamber of Deputies. The grounds for impeachment include crimes such as corruption, abuse of power, and violations of the Constitution. After the Chamber's approval, the case is then sent to the Senate, which conducts a trial. If convicted, the President can be removed from office
National Assembly could vote for no confidence against the Prime Minister and the council with having a majority as the criteria

Sweden has clear procedures for dismissing the executive for violations of the law. The Prime Minister can face a vote of no confidence or judicial review, ensuring accountability for illegal actions.

Executive Structure: In Egypt, the executive branch is headed by the President, who is both the head of state and head of government. The President appoints the Prime Minister and the Cabinet. Presidential Selection Process: 1. Elections: Presidents are officially chosen through national elections. 2. Term length: Following constitutional amendments in 2019, presidential terms are 6 years long. 3. Term limits: A president can serve up to two terms. Fairness Concerns: The fairness of the executive selection process in Egypt has been subject to significant criticism and debate: 1. Electoral integrity: International observers and human rights organizations have raised concerns about the fairness and transparency of elections. Issues include: - Restrictions on opposition candidates - Limited media freedom - Allegations of voter intimidation 2. Opposition suppression: There have been reports of opposition figures facing legal challenges or arrests, limiting genuine competition. 3. 2018 Election controversy: The 2018 presidential election, which saw incumbent Abdel Fattah el-Sisi re-elected, was particularly controversial. Several potential candidates withdrew or were disqualified, leaving only one nominal opponent. 4. Constitutional amendments: The 2019 constitutional changes that extended term limits were passed through a referendum, but critics argued the process was rushed and lacked genuine public debate. 5. Military influence: The significant role of the military in Egyptian politics raises questions about civilian control over the executive. Executive Arbitration: In the Egyptian context, executive arbitration often refers to the president's power to mediate conflicts and make decisive rulings. This includes: 1. Decree powers: The president can issue decrees with the force of law, especially when parliament is not in session. 2. Emergency powers: Under a state of emergency (which has been frequently renewed), the executive has expanded powers to detain individuals and restrict civil liberties. 3. Appointments: The president appoints key positions including governors, heads of independent bodies, and top military commanders. 4. Foreign policy: The president has significant autonomy in conducting foreign policy and making international agreements. Challenges to Fairness: 1. Centralization of power: Critics argue that too much power is concentrated in the executive, particularly the presidency. 2. Weak checks and balances: The judiciary and legislature are often seen as unable to effectively check executive power. 3. Lack of transparency: Decision-making processes within the executive branch are often opaque. 4. Selective application: There are concerns about the selective application of laws and regulations to favor government allies and target opponents. It's important to note that supporters of the government argue that strong executive powers are necessary for stability and to address security and economic challenges. However, many domestic and international observers continue to express concerns about the fairness and inclusiveness of Egypt's political system, particularly regarding executive power and selection.

questions.text: Is it possible to remove the executive for major political failures in managing the economy or foreign affairs?
questions.info_link: If the executive mismanagement of the country can not be immediately held accountable in any way, rate low. Also rate low if the courts or legislature repeatedly remove executives for minor trumped-up mistakes. If removal of the executive for mismanagement is possible, though not necessarily frequent, rate high.

comments.comment: In Georgia, it is possible to remove the executive for major political failures, including those related to economic management or foreign affairs. The Prime Minister and government can be subjected to a vote of no confidence by the Parliament, which can lead to their dismissal. This mechanism allows for accountability when significant political failures occur.

the executive can be removed through a process called impeachment. This typically requires evidence of crimes such as malfeasance or violation of the Constitution. Political failures, like mismanagement of the economy or foreign affairs, alone may not be sufficient grounds for impeachment, but if they are tied to illegal actions or significant breaches of duty, they could potentially be grounds for removal. The process involves both the Chamber of Deputies and the Senate, with a two-thirds majority required for impeachment to proceed.

It is possible to remove the executive in Sweden for political failures, such as mismanagement of the economy or foreign affairs. The Riksdag has the authority to pass a vote of no confidence, leading to the government's resignation.

questions.text: Rate the extent to which executive actions can be challenged in the courts.
questions.info_link: A low rating implies an insufficient judicial recourse, while a high rating denotes an adequate recourse.

comments.comment: While the Prime Minister of Georgia can serve indefinitely, as long as they are nominated by Parliament after elections, introducing clearer term limits for this role may strengthen democratic turnover and limit the concentration of power. Enhancing the independence of the judiciary can ensure that executive actions are effectively checked without political interference.

In Brazil, executive actions can be challenged in the courts to a significant extent. The judiciary has the authority to review actions taken by the executive for legality and constitutional compliance. Individuals and entities can file lawsuits, particularly through mechanisms like "Mandado de Seguranša" to protect their rights against unlawful acts. Additionally, the Supreme Federal Court (STF) plays a crucial role in adjudicating disputes involving executive actions, ensuring accountability and adherence to the law. However, the effectiveness of these challenges can vary based on political context and judicial independence. Overall, while there are robust mechanisms for judicial review, practical outcomes depend on various factors.
Executive actions in Sweden can be challenged in the courts. The judiciary serves as an independent body that reviews executive decisions, ensuring they comply with the constitution and laws.

questions.text: To what extent are organizations representing business, labour, farmers and other social groups consulted about policies that affect their interests?
questions.info_link: Rate high if adequate consultation is sought from all interested parties, rate low if a narrow spectrum of interests contribute to decisions, and set at 4 if the executive does not seek any input.

comments.comment: Sweden maintains wide consultation with various social groups, including business, labor, and farmers, when formulating policies. However, there are occasional criticisms regarding insufficient representation of certain interest groups.
there is the National Council of Tripartite Cooperation that includes govt representatives, trade unions, and employee associations. There is also advisory councils for other social groups.

questions.text: Rate the extent to which administrative personnel are selected on the basis of merit.
questions.info_link:
Low ratings denote poor selection processes, or good processes that are inconsistently applied. High ratings indicate a sound, consistent selection process.
For the purposes of this exercise, please consider the term "Public Administration" to include all non-elected employees of the state, in all of its forms. This includes police, fire fighters, ambulence crews, department administrators and staff, civil and foreign service members, the postal service, military services and all other public employees who are not directly elected. Ideally, all members of the public administration are selected on the basis of merit, save for a few members of the staff of political appointees.
Unfortunately, this is not the case in many countries. Where patronage, nepotism, favoritism and networking are the only avenues to public employment, more qualified individuals will be left behind. This leads to increased corruption in several ways. First of all, the fight against corruption will suffer with thse less qualified individuals at the helm. Secondly, the direct link between patronage and advancement may lead public employees to seek advancement through illicit favors, rather than performance. In addition, a corrupt public agency which uses non-meritous hiring procedures will invariably select employees based on their likelihood of contributing to or at least turning their back on the corrupt behavior of incumbent employees. For more information on merit-based public employment, see the World Bank's discussion on the topic at http://www1.worldbank.org/publicsector/civilservice/recruitment.htm/. The record of Georgia is a clear example in cronyist appointment, even since the Velvet Revolution and its anti-corruption element. See a related article at http://admin.corisweb.org/index.php?fuseaction=news.view&id=111725&src=pub

comments.comment: This is because while Georgia has established a merit-based system legally, there are ongoing challenges and areas for improvement in its implementation. For example, certain sectors, like higher education, face inconsistencies in systematically applying merit-based hiring practices.

Sweden follows a meritocratic system where public administrative personnel are selected based on their qualifications, skills, and performance. Recruitment processes are highly transparent and fair, ensuring that selection is almost always based on merit.

2 types of employees: civil servants and public employees through labour contracts. Even though there is open competition in appointment of civil servants, there are loops like greater political control over appointments especially in senior level positions

questions.text: To what extent do agencies administering most programmes of government change when there are big changes in those in control of government?
questions.info_link: If a big change occurs in the government, one should expect a similar change in the agencies these newly empowered politicians oversee. Rate high if this is the case, but rate low if a big change in government leads to little or no change in the public administration, suggesting 'entrenched' employees which may be too powerful to manage effectively.

comments.comment: Despite various reforms, the public administration still remains centralized (A colonial legacy)

Swedenĺs administrative agencies are designed to remain stable and are rarely affected by changes in political control. There are few personnel changes in public administration after major political shifts, ensuring continuity in governance.

In Russia, when there are significant changes in those who control the government, the agencies administering most government programs tend to experience moderate changes rather than drastic changes. While top leadership positions within these agencies may be replaced to align with new priorities, the core structures and personnel often remain the same. This stability is partly due to the entrenched bureaucracy and the centralized nature of Russian governance. As a result, policy directions might shift, but the fundamental operations of the agencies usually continue without major disruptions.

questions.text: Is there a clear chain of responsibility for tasks within public administration?
questions.info_link: If the administration is organized haphazardly, rate low; if there is a clear hierarchy and sufficient oversight, rate high.

comments.comment: Indonesian bureaucracy is divided into administrative positions (managerial roles) and functional positions (subject matter experts), along with a standard hierarchical structure within the bureaucracy.
Power is highly centralized in the hands of the DRC government instead of the local or provincial government.

Sweden has a clear and well-defined hierarchy within its public administration, ensuring accountability and responsibility for tasks. The chain of command is transparent and easy to follow across different levels of administration.

In Russia's public administration, there is a formal chain of responsibility that outlines tasks and hierarchies within government agencies. On paper, roles and responsibilities are clearly defined, following a centralized and hierarchical structure typical of many governmental systems. However, in practice, this chain of responsibility isn't always as clear-cut. Bureaucratic complexities and overlapping jurisdictions can blur the lines of accountability. Sometimes, multiple agencies may be involved in similar tasks without clear coordination, leading to confusion over who is ultimately responsible. So, while there is a structured chain of responsibility in theory, its effectiveness can be hindered by practical challenges, making it less clear in everyday operations. sources : russia country report
Works in hierarchical principal with each unit having its own set of regulations for work ( Administrative law)

questions.text: Rate the fairness of terms of employment for public employees, including entry, deployment, promotion, salaries and dismissal.
questions.info_link: If the process is arbitrary or unfair, rate low; if the process is fair and consistent, rate high.

comments.comment: It is rather arbitrary and marked by political interests of the state. For instance, the military has replaced civilian authorities in North Kivu and Ituri- both of which are centers experiencing political and ethnic violence.

The terms of employment for public employees in Sweden are fair, covering aspects such as entry, promotion, salary, and dismissal. Public employees benefit from strong labor protections, and decisions on employment are based on objective criteria.

Officially, civil service positions are intended to be filled through competitive exams and merit-based selection. However, in practice, nepotism and personal connections often play a significant role in hiring decisions. This makes it harder for qualified candidates without the right networks to enter public service fairly. Career advancement within the public sector can depend more on loyalty to superiors and adherence to political expectations than on merit or performance. Patronage systems and favoritism can overshadow objective assessments, limiting fair promotion opportunities for many employees. While there are standardized salary scales, discrepancies exist. Some positions come with unofficial perks or allowances, and regional variations can lead to unequal pay for similar roles. Generally, public sector salaries are lower compared to the private sector, which can affect morale and fairness perceptions. Although there are legal protections for public employees, enforcement is inconsistent. Employees may face dismissal for reasons unrelated to job performance, such as political disagreements or organizational restructuring, often with limited avenues for redress. sources: OECD public governance reviews

Egypt's administrative structure and the fairness of personnel appointments are complex topics with several layers to consider. Here's an overview: Administrative Structure: Egypt has a centralized administrative system divided into: 1. Central government 2. Governorates (27 in total) 3. Districts 4. Cities and villages Key aspects of this structure include: 1. Centralization: Most significant decisions are made at the national level, with limited local autonomy. 2. Presidential appointments: The President appoints governors and other key local officials. 3. Local councils: While local councils exist, they have limited power compared to appointed officials. Fairness in Appointments: The fairness of personnel appointments in Egypt's administrative structure is often questioned due to several factors: 1. Patronage networks: Appointments often rely heavily on personal and political connections rather than merit. 2. Lack of transparency: The criteria and processes for many appointments are not clearly defined or publicly accessible. 3. Political loyalty: There's a perception that political loyalty to the ruling regime is a significant factor in appointments, especially for higher positions. 4. Military influence: Former military officers frequently receive appointments to key civilian administrative positions, raising concerns about the militarization of the bureaucracy. 5. Limited rotation: Many high-level positions see little turnover, potentially stifling new ideas and perpetuating existing power structures. 6. Nepotism and corruption: There are frequent allegations of nepotism and corruption in the appointment process. 7. Gender imbalance: Despite some progress, women remain underrepresented in high-level administrative positions. 8. Centralized control: The centralized nature of appointments means local communities often have little say in who governs them. Attempts at Reform: There have been some efforts to improve the fairness of the administrative structure and appointments: 1. Civil service law: A 2015 law aimed to reform the civil service, including changes to hiring and promotion practices. However, implementation has been slow and inconsistent. 2. Anti-corruption initiatives: The government has launched various anti-corruption campaigns, though their effectiveness and impartiality are debated. 3. Women's representation: There have been efforts to increase women's representation in administrative roles, including a quota system for the parliament. 4. Training programs: Initiatives to improve the skills and professionalism of civil servants have been introduced. Challenges to Fairness: 1. Weak accountability mechanisms: There's a lack of robust, independent oversight of administrative appointments and performance. 2. Limited public participation: Citizens have few formal channels to influence administrative appointments or hold officials accountable. 3. Institutional inertia: Long-standing practices and entrenched interests make systemic reform challenging. 4. Political context: The broader political environment, with limited space for opposition and civil society, impacts the fairness of administrative processes. In conclusion, while Egypt has a structured administrative system, the fairness of appointments and the overall functioning of this system face significant challenges. The centralized nature of power, the influence of personal and political connections, and the lack of transparency in many processes contribute to perceptions of unfairness. While there have been some reform efforts, many observers argue that more comprehensive changes are needed to ensure a truly fair and effective administrative structure.

questions.text: Are experienced high-level public administrators consulted by politicians about the preparation of public policies?
questions.info_link: If these experts are nearly always consulted, rate high. If public administrators are rarely consulted or if their advice is always ignored, rate low.

comments.comment: Experienced high-level public administrators in Sweden are frequently consulted by politicians when developing public policies. This collaborative approach ensures informed decision-making and continuity in governance.

In Russia, experienced high-level public administrators are consulted by politicians during the preparation of public policies to a moderate extent. These administrators bring valuable technical expertise and institutional knowledge that can inform policy decisions. Ministries and government agencies often draft policy proposals and provide analyses that feed into the legislative process.However, the policy-making process is highly centralized, with significant influence from the executive branch, particularly the President and the Presidential Administration. Political considerations and directives from top leadership can overshadow the input from public administrators. This centralization means that while consultations occur, the final policy decisions may not fully reflect the administrators' expertise or recommendations.also, the overlap between political and administrative roles can blur the lines of consultation. Some high-level administrators may also hold political positions or be closely aligned with political leaders, which affects the dynamics of policy preparation. sources: OECD public governance reviews

questions.text: Are there transparent procedures for consultation and cooperation between different institutions sharing responsibility for government programmes?
questions.info_link: Rate high if administrative authority is clearly delineated, disputes are rare and easily resolved; rate low if there are many overlapping areas of authority and recurrent, contentious disputes between agencies.

comments.comment: Georgia's commitment to the Open Government Partnership (OGP) reflects its ongoing efforts to improve transparency in government operations, including sharing responsibilities among various institutions. The recently approved OGP Action Plan for 2024-2025 is a significant step toward enhancing cooperation and ensuring that processes are open to public scrutiny

There are transparent procedures in place for inter-institutional cooperation in Sweden. Different government bodies and agencies coordinate effectively when administering programs, reflecting a high level of organizational transparency.

We think that procedures for consultation and cooperation between different government institutions sharing responsibility for government programs exist but are often not fully transparent. While formal mechanisms are in place to facilitate inter-agency collaborationŚsuch as interdepartmental committees and working groupsŚtheir operations are frequently opaque to the public and even to other parts of the government. Bureaucratic hurdles and a hierarchical administrative culture can impede open communication and effective cooperation. Agencies might withhold information to maintain control over certain policy areas, leading to silos and fragmented implementation of government programs. Overlapping mandates without clear guidelines on coordination can further complicate collaboration efforts. Transparency is also affected by the centralized nature of the Russian government. Major decisions are often made at higher levels of power, with limited input from all the institutions involved. This top-down approach can bypass established procedures for consultation, reducing the opportunities for cooperative policymaking. Efforts have been made to improve inter-agency cooperation and transparency, such as the introduction of e-government initiatives aimed at streamlining communication and sharing information. However, these initiatives have had limited impact due to resistance within the bureaucracy and a lack of enforcement mechanisms to ensure compliance with transparency regulations. sources: World bank
Transperancy is made possible through right to access public information. However there is no dedicated public body to supervise implementation and this access varies based on the institution

questions.text: Rate the extent to which administrative regulations are applied fairly.
questions.info_link: Rate high if rules are easy to understand, transparent, and consistently and objectively applied. Rate low if rules are obscure, secretive or applied inconsistently.

comments.comment: The application of regulations is very unfair. For instance, the public administration is characterized by lax tax regulations that facilitate embezzlement of millions of dollars. Lack of transparency regarding tax regulations often lead to informal and illegal collection of taxes from the citizens.
Administrative regulations in Sweden are applied fairly and are highly transparent. Public administration is subject to rigorous oversight to ensure that rules are applied consistently and equitably.
The extent to which administrative regulations are applied fairly in Russia is relatively low due to several systemic issues that hinder consistent and impartial enforcement.

questions.text: What is the proximity and accessibility of administrative systems to the people they are meant to service?
questions.info_link: If public services and administrative systems are relatively remote, give a low score. If these services are if responsive, give a high score.

comments.comment: The implementation of digital solutions, such as platforms like my.gov.ge, has significantly enhanced accessibility and convenience for many citizens in Georgia. However, the necessity for individuals to travel to administrative centers, particularly in rural areas, poses substantial challenges to accessing these services. While various reforms have been introduced to improve the public administration system, these efforts are still ongoing and encounter numerous hurdles, indicating that there is considerable room for further improvement.

Little to no state presence in rural Congo has rendered accessibility to any administrative support impossible. It is marred by lack of access to basic facilities like water, sanitation and healthcare

Swedenĺs administrative systems are highly accessible to the public. Citizens can easily access administrative services, and agencies are responsive to the needs of the people they are meant to serve.

Russia's immense size and the remote locations of many settlements make it challenging to provide accessible administrative services nationwide. Rural areas often lack local government offices, requiring residents to travel long distances for basic services.Administrative systems are highly centralized, with significant decision-making power concentrated in federal or regional capitals. Local administrations may have limited authority or resources to address community needs effectively.Despite efforts to improve the situation through technological advancements and service centers, significant barriers remain. Geographic challenges, infrastructural deficiencies, bureaucratic hurdles, and socio-economic disparities contribute to the limited proximity and accessibility of administrative systems for many Russians. sources: UNDP Russia

questions.text: Rate the extent to which administrative agencies are sufficiently funded to carry out their mandates.
questions.info_link: with adequately paid employees merit a high rating, while administrations facing woeful shortages of resources warrant a low rating.

comments.comment: Georgia has made notable strides in budget transparency, ranking first out of 121 countries in the 2023 Open Budget Survey, with a score of 87, indicating a commitment to accountable financial practices . Funding for administrative agencies is unclear. Source:https://www.geostat.ge/en

Administrative agencies in Sweden are adequately funded to carry out their mandates. The government ensures sufficient resources for public administration, enabling agencies to function effectively and deliver services to citizens.

While there are ongoing efforts to address funding issues, many administrative agencies in Russia remain underfunded. Economic factors, budgetary priorities, regional inequalities, and systemic corruption contribute to the challenges these agencies face in fulfilling their mandates effectively.Russia's economy is heavily reliant on oil and gas revenues. Fluctuations in global energy prices can lead to unpredictable government revenues, affecting budget allocations to administrative agencies. Economic sanctions and geopolitical tensions have also impacted government income, leading to budget tightening.A significant portion of the federal budget is allocated to defense, security, and large-scale national projects. This can result in reduced funding for administrative agencies responsible for social services, education, healthcare, and local governance.Priorities set by the central government may not align with the needs of regional agencies, leading to mismatches in resource allocation. Misappropriation of funds due to corruption reduces the resources available for agencies to fulfill their mandates. Inefficient management practices and bureaucratic overheads can lead to wasteful spending. sources: Tranparency International Russia

questions.text: Rate the extent to which administrative agencies are free of influence by criminal elements.
questions.info_link: Rate high if free of criminal influence, rate low if the bureaucracy is ridden with criminal elements.

These groups may infiltrate administrative agencies to gain access to sensitive information, control over regulatory processes, or to protect their interests.
